retrieved_docs.title: Acute Anemia -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), haptoglobin, and bilirubin can indicate hemolytic anemia, with elevated LDH, indirect bilirubin, and decreased haptoglobin levels. Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) levels are commonly elevated in patients with upper GI bleeds due to undigested blood. An increased reticulocyte count may be noted secondary to an erythropoietic response by the bone marrow, suggesting active RBC production. Conversely, a low reticulocyte count may indicate an inadequate bone marrow response, as seen in conditions such as aplastic anemia, hematologic cancers, and drug or toxin exposures. Reticulocytosis often corresponds with an increased MCV in the blood count.

retrieved_docs.title: Pyruvate Kinase Deficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory evaluation is indicated for neonatal anemia or hyperbilirubinemia. Childhood chronic anemia with splenomegaly warrants investigation. Transfusion-dependent patients require evaluation. [4]  Complete blood count characterizes anemia. Hemoglobin levels vary for different severities of PK deficiency. A blood smear shows normochromic cells. Polychromasia with echinocytes may be seen. Spherocytes are absent. Macrocytosis indicates folate deficiency. [3] Reticulocytosis does not correlate with hemolysis. [9] LDH is elevated, and haptoglobin is low. Coombs' test and osmotic fragility are negative. Hemoglobin electrophoresis is normal.

retrieved_docs.title: Normochromic Normocytic Anemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Reticulocyte count must be obtained to determine the pathophysiologic mechanism of anemia. Increased reticulocyte count demonstrates hemolysis; other evidence related to increased RBC destruction, eg, increased LDH, increased indirect bilirubin level, decreased haptoglobin, etc, should also be considered. A decreased reticulocyte count is associated with a hypoproliferative state like aplastic anemias, kidney disease, hypothyroidism, etc. Corrected reticulocyte count = percent reticulocytes x (patient's hematocrit/normal hematocrit) Peripheral smear - Erythrocyte morphology (shape and size; absence of schistocytes rules out microangiopathic hemolysis; microspherocytes indicate hemolysis; rouleaux formation relates to multiple myeloma. Lactate dehydrogenase, haptoglobin, bilirubin - to rule out hemolysis Iron panel (iron, ferritin, total iron binding capacity, transferrin saturation) [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Anemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Although a patient may present with physical characteristics that may lead a clinician down the path to diagnosing hemolytic anemia, laboratory markers are key. Results that will help confirm hemolysis are an elevated reticulocyte count, increased lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), elevated unconjugated bilirubin, and decreased Haptoglobin.

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Elliptocytosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The evaluation of hereditary elliptocytosis is initiated with a comprehensive assessment, including a complete blood count (CBC) revealing normocytic normochromic anemia. A peripheral blood smear is essential, typically demonstrating elliptocytes in 15% to 100% of RBCs, along with other variations, such as spherocytes, stomatocytes, poikilocytes, ovalocytes, and macro-ovalocytes. Testing for hemolysis reveals features of extravascular hemolysis characterized by an elevated reticulocyte count, increased lactate dehydrogenase, indirect bilirubin, and decreased haptoglobin level. The predominance of elliptocytes does not consistently correlate with the severity of hemolysis. Polychromasia is expected with hemolysis and reticulocytosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Rh Hemolytic Disease(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Other important causes for this physiopathology are ABO incompatibility, hereditary spherocytosis, hereditary enzyme deficiencies, fetomaternal hemorrhage, twin transfusion syndrome, thalassemia, especially alpha thalassemia, and thrombotic microangiopathies. Investigations to differentiate these conditions from Rh-incompatibility are complete blood count with differential, peripheral blood smear, conjugated and unconjugated bilirubin levels, liver function tests (serum alanine aminotransferase, aspartate aminotransferase, gamma-glutamyltransferase, alkaline phosphatase, and albumin level), serum blood markers to differentiate intravascular hemolysis causes from extravascular causes (e.g., lactate dehydrogenase level, hemosiderin level in blood and urine, haptoglobin level), reticulocyte count, RDW (red cell distribution width), and direct and indirect Coomb's test.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: The information below should be included in the laboratory report for hemolytic anemia. Patient information: Name, age, sex, and relevant medical history. Laboratory results: Results of CBC, peripheral blood smear interpretation, reticulocyte count, haptoglobin, LDH, bilirubin, Coombs test, and osmotic fragility test. Interpretation: Analysis of the results in the context of hemolytic anemia, including any findings to pinpoint the underlying cause. Recommendations: Further tests or consultations are recommended based on the findings, such as genetic testing for hereditary hemolytic disorders or additional imaging studies. Critical findings in the laboratory evaluation of hemolytic anemia are mentioned below.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Anemia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A patient with anemia may present with shortness of breath, weakness, fatigue, arrhythmias such as tachycardia, or can present asymptomatic. Those with anemia caused by cell destruction or hemolysis may also present with jaundice or hematuria. If symptoms have been going on for longer periods, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, cholestasis may even be seen.

retrieved_docs.title: Blood Transfusion -- Complications -- Fatal Hemolysis
retrieved_docs.content: Retype and crossmatch Direct and indirect Coombs tests Complete blood count (CBC), creatinine, PT, and PTT (draw from the other arm) Peripheral smear Haptoglobin, indirect bilirubin, LDH, plasma-free hemoglobin Urinalysis for hemoglobin

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Uroporphyrinogen -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolysis - Due to the accumulation of porphyrins within erythrocytes, intravascular hemolysis is a major feature of CEP. In an effort to compensate, the bone marrow and spleen actually increase the production of heme and hemoglobin synthesis, although it is usually not enough to counter the amount of hemolysis/ineffective erythropoiesis. This leads to typical findings of hemolytic anemia with splenomegaly(extramedullary erythropoiesis), increased reticulocyte count, increased indirect bilirubin, and decreased haptoglobin. Blood smear typically reveals nucleated RBCs, basophilic stippling, anisocytosis, poikilocytosis, and polychromasia. In severe cases, the hemolysis may cause hydrops fetalis in utero or jaundice in the neonatal period.

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Steps to evaluate for hemolytic anemia 1) Confirm the presence of hemolysis- elevated LDH, corrected reticulocyte count >2%, elevated indirect bilirubin and decreased/low haptoglobin 2) Determine extra vs. intravascular hemolysis- Extravascular Spherocytes present Urine hemosiderin negative Urine hemoglobin negative Intravascular Urine hemosiderin elevated Urine hemoglobin elevated 3) Examine the peripheral blood smear [9] Spherocytes: immune hemolytic anemia (Direct antiglobulin test DAT+) vs. hereditary spherocytosis (DAT-) Bite cells: G6PD deficiency Target cells: hemoglobinopathy or liver disease Schistocytes: TTP/HUS, DIC, prosthetic valve, malignant HTN Acanthocytes: liver disease Parasitic inclusions: malaria, babesiosis, bartonellosis 4) If spherocytes +, check if DAT is + DAT(+): Immune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) DAT (-): Hereditary spherocytosis

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Spherocytosis  -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The classic triad of jaundice, anemia, and splenomegaly is rare in neonates. The most common finding in this population is jaundice. Thus hereditary spherocytosis should be considered in neonates with hyperbilirubinemia of unknown cause. Additionally, spherocytes are seen less often in newborns along with several markers of the hemolysis that are typically present in older infants or adults. [9] Most neonates with hereditary spherocytosis have a positive family history; this should increase suspicion and aid in the diagnostic workup of patients with an unknown cause of jaundice. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia Screening -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolysis profile: This profile includes haptoglobin, LDH, and indirect bilirubin, which help evaluate RBC destruction.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemoglobin C Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In the patients with the hemoglobin C trait, hemoglobin concentrations are usually within the low normal to the normal range. The life span of red cells gets decreased but despite this, the reticulocyte counts are not increased. The peripheral smear may show target cells and intracellular crystals. Likewise, the patients with Hemoglobin C disease, as mentioned earlier, show the lab findings suggestive of hemolysis: increased LDH, reticulocyte count, and direct bilirubin. The peripheral smear shows microcytosis, a large number of target cells, spherocytes, and crystallized hemoglobin.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Severe anemia: Hemoglobin levels below a critical threshold necessitate prompt intervention to avoid complications such as tissue hypoxia. High reticulocyte count: Indicates active bone marrow response to hemolysis, suggesting RBC destruction. Significantly elevated LDH: Suggests extensive hemolysis and tissue damage, requiring immediate investigation and management. Positive direct Coombs test: Indicates immune-mediated hemolysis, which may require specific treatment modalities such as immunosuppressive therapy. Characteristic morphological changes on peripheral blood smear: Presence of schistocytes, spherocytes, or other abnormal RBC morphologies indicative of specific hemolytic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Anemia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: One of the main laboratory values that aids in the diagnosis of hemolytic anemia is an elevated reticulocyte count, as the bone marrow is attempting to produce increased amounts of RBCs. This can be seen in other disease processes such as blood loss anemia; therefore, one must be cautious in taking a thorough history as well as evaluating other lab values that should also be altered, including LDH, haptoglobin, and indirect bilirubin.

Recognizing these interconnections, the associated syndromes, and the relevant laboratory parameters (e.g., total hemoglobin) is essential for accurate diagnosis, interpretation of test results, and targeted management of infants during the neonatal and early infancy periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 284
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice are medical conditions that most frequently appear during the fetal and/or neonatal period and are especially prevalent in infancy, a specific stage within the broader period of life between birth and death. These conditions are synonymous with the Arabic term “يرقان وليدي” (neonatal jaundice) and are closely linked to Rh immunoprophylaxis (“تمنيع راهائي إِسوِي Rh”), reflecting the role of Rh incompatibility in their pathogenesis. Laboratory evaluation of hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice relies on the measurement of total hemoglobin concentration and the red blood cell count (“عدد خلايا الدم الحمراء”), which are used to interpret the severity of the condition. When such test results fall below the reference range, this is interpreted as an indicator of hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice. Although the condition can be observed throughout the lifespan, its primary occurrence is in the early stages of life, underscoring the importance of early detection and appropriate management in newborns and infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1328
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice are clinical entities that typically occur during the fetal and/or neonatal period and are commonly referred to in Arabic as “يرقان وليدي” (neonatal jaundice). When evaluating a newborn with suspected hemolytic jaundice, laboratory measurements such as total hemoglobin concentration and red blood cell count (عدد خلايا الدم الحمراء) are essential; values that fall below the reference range are interpreted as indicative of hemolysis contributing to the jaundice. Although hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice are not synonymous with Rh immunoprophylaxis (تمنيع راهائي إِسوِي Rh), they are closely related because Rh immunoprophylaxis is a preventive strategy that reduces the risk of Rh‑mediated hemolytic disease of the newborn, a major cause of severe neonatal jaundice. Integrating the timing of onset, laboratory interpretation, and preventive measures such as Rh immunoprophylaxis is crucial for accurate diagnosis and effective management of hemolytic jaundice in the newborn.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 228
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice, also referred to by the Arabic term “يرقان وليدي,” can occur during the fetal and/or neonatal period and may be observed throughout the broader period of life between birth and death, including the specific stage of infancy. In clinical assessment, a total hemoglobin concentration that falls below the reference range is interpreted as indicative of hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice, and the measurement of total hemoglobin concentration is used to interpret the presence of this condition. Recognizing the timing of occurrence, the laboratory interpretation of low hemoglobin, and the synonymous terminology enhances patient evaluation and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2328
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice are conditions that typically arise during the fetal and/or neonatal period. They are closely linked to the Arabic term “يرقان وليدي,” which denotes neonatal jaundice, indicating that these entities represent the same clinical phenomenon of jaundice occurring in newborns. The condition is also related and possibly synonymous with “تمنيع راهائي إِسوِي Rh,” suggesting an association with Rh‑mediated hemolytic disease of the newborn. In the laboratory context, measurements of total hemoglobin concentration are interpreted in light of hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice; when hemoglobin values fall below the reference range, this finding is considered an important interpretive clue for the presence of hemolytic or perinatal jaundice. Recognizing these interconnections aids clinicians in diagnosing and managing jaundice in the newborn period, especially when Rh alloimmunization is a contributing factor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 105
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice are conditions that typically occur in the fetal and/or neonatal period and are closely related to the Arabic‑language concept “يرقان وليدي” (neonatal jaundice). Evaluation of these jaundice types relies on the measurement of total hemoglobin concentration, which is used to interpret the clinical significance of the findings. In this context, a laboratory result that falls below the reference range is interpreted as an important indicator when assessing hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice in newborns.

Laboratory findings that fall below reference ranges, particularly low total hemoglobin concentration, are interpreted as indicative of hemolytic or perinatal jaundice, underscoring the diagnostic importance of quantitative testing in this age group. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that the neonatal period serves as a critical temporal nexus linking infectious, metabolic, hematologic, and hemorrhagic conditions—many organized under “other perinatal conditions” or “hemolytic‑jaundice” categories—and that recognizing these interconnections is essential for accurate assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 150
retrieved_docs.content: (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان); and * Jaundice specifically caused by Rh alloimmunization in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد). These jaundice subtypes are often evaluated together with total hemoglobin concentration, where values below the reference range support the diagnosis of hemolytic disease. Recognizing the interrelated nature of neonatal infections, genetic syndromes, hemolytic processes, diverse jaundice mechanisms and hemorrhagic events—along with appropriate laboratory interpretation—is essential for accurate diagnosis, timely intervention and optimal care of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 917
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified under neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. This broader category encompasses several specific neonatal conditions, including نزف سري بعد الولادة (umbilical hemorrhage after birth), نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (respiratory tract hemorrhage in the newborn), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), and يرقان نووي (physiologic neonatal jaundice). Together, these entities represent key hemorrhagic and hyperbilirubinemic presentations that are important for the diagnosis, monitoring, and management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 987
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice are clinical entities that commonly arise during the fetal and/or neonatal period and can continue throughout infancy, a stage that lies within the broader period of life between birth and death. These conditions are also known by the Arabic term “يرقان وليدي,” reflecting their cross‑linguistic recognition. Laboratory assessment frequently includes measurement of total hemoglobin concentration, and results that fall below the reference range are interpreted as supportive of hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice. The disorders are closely related to, and may be synonymous with, Rh alloimmunization (تمنيع راهائي إِسوِي Rh), underscoring an immuno‑hematologic mechanism of red‑cell destruction. Recognizing the timing of occurrence, the significance of low hemoglobin values, and the link to Rh alloimmunization is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective management of affected newborns and infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1802
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this hemoglobin disorder commonly manifests as نقص الأكسجين في الدم, indicating reduced oxygen levels in the blood.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2812
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, which is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hereditary hemoglobin disorder that can precipitate serious respiratory complications in adults. Clinically, داء الكريات المنجلية is associated with respiratory failure, respiratory insufficiency, and adult cardiac arrest, underscoring the need for vigilant respiratory monitoring and prompt intervention in patients affected by this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1081
retrieved_docs.content: NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN is a complex neonatal condition that gives rise to several related complications. One important manifestation is neonatal jaundice that results from delayed conjugation due to the immature development of the hepatic conjugating system. In addition, the condition includes نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage), which reflects the hemorrhagic component of the disease. Various forms of neonatal jaundice are also linked to this disorder: يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice) in general, يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (neonatal jaundice caused by hemolysis of the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (jaundice associated with preterm birth). Together, these interrelated entities illustrate how hemorrhagic and hemolytic processes in the newborn can impair liver function, lead to delayed bilirubin conjugation, and produce respiratory bleeding, underscoring the need for comprehensive assessment and coordinated management of affected infants.
